message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
export the schema and data for table schema_migrations

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- ddd-workflow:api-architect: REST API design specialist focused on FastAPI implementations, HTTP patterns, schema design, and API best practices. Proactively reviews API designs and ensures consistent REST patterns. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:ba-analyst: Stage 1 Business Analyst that creates UC documents (DUC/BUC/PROC) using skill templates, adds stakeholder analysis, business impact assessment, and proactive alternative flow suggestions. Can ask clarifying questions about requirements. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write)

- ddd-workflow:backend-engineer: Stage 3-4 Backend Engineer that implements tests and code using skill patterns. Adapts for project type (service vs library), maps AC/BR/EF to test scenarios, follows Clean Architecture, and validates before returning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:clean-arch-enforcer: Clean Architecture specialist focused on maintaining architectural boundaries, dependency inversion, and layer separation. Proactively enforces architectural constraints and prevents violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-architect: Domain-Driven Design expert for exploring existing codebases and designing new domain models. Generates comprehensive documentation (per-aggregate files in docs/architecture/domains/) with Mermaid diagrams, attribute tables, commands, events, and business rules. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-engineer: Stage 2 Domain Engineer that designs domain models, aggregates, commands, and events using skill patterns. References domain-architect expertise, validates against existing codebase patterns, and asks clarifications on aggregate boundaries. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit)

- ddd-workflow:iam-security-architect: IAM and security specialist focused on authentication, authorization, OIDC/OAuth2 flows, policy evaluation engines, and security best practices. Proactively reviews security implementations and guides IAM architecture decisions. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:reviewer: Stage 5-6 reviewer that runs tests, validates AC/BR/EF coverage, orchestrates aspect validators in parallel, and generates unified review reports. Can ask clarifying questions about violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:task-planner: Brainstorm and break down high-level feature requests into actionable tasks with size estimates. Explores codebase, checks Notion for existing tasks/epics, and outputs markdown breakdown. Can ask clarifying questions about scope and priorities. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)
- devops:repo-grouper:repo-grouper: Orchestrates git submodule discovery, commit collection, and release notes generation for monorepos (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- understand-anything:architecture-analyzer: Analyzes a codebase's file structure, summaries, and import relationships to identify
logical architectural layers and assign every file to exactly one layer. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:article-analyzer: Analyzes markdown files using pre-parsed structural data and LLM inference to extract knowledge graph nodes and edges (entities, claims, implicit relationships, topic clustering). (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:assemble-reviewer: Reviews the output of merge-batch-graphs.py for semantic issues the script
cannot catch. Recovers dropped nodes/edges and fills cross-batch gaps. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:domain-analyzer: Analyzes codebases to extract business domain knowledge — domains, business flows, and process steps. Produces a domain-graph.json that maps how business logic flows through the code. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:file-analyzer: Analyzes batches of source files to produce knowledge graph nodes and edges.
Extracts file structure, functions, classes, and relationships using a two-phase
approach: structural extraction script followed by LLM semantic analysis. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:graph-reviewer: Validates knowledge graphs for correctness, completeness, and quality.
Runs systematic checks and renders approval or rejection decisions. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:knowledge-graph-guide: Use this agent when users need help understanding, querying, or working
with an Understand-Anything knowledge graph. Guides users through graph
structure, node/edge relationships, layer architecture, tours, and
dashboard usage. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:project-scanner: Scans a codebase directory to produce a structured inventory of all project files,
detected languages, frameworks, import maps, and estimated complexity. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:tour-builder: Designs guided learning tours through codebases, creating 5-15 pedagogical steps
that teach project architecture and key concepts in logical order. (Tools: All tools)

addedBlocks:
## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Tools: get_editor_state, open_document, get_guidelines, batch_get, batch_design, snapshot_layout, get_screenshot, get_variables, set_variables, find_empty_space_on_canvas, search_all_unique_properties, replace_all_matching_properties, export_nodes.

## plugin:personal-os:notebooklm-mcp
NotebookLM MCP - Access NotebookLM (notebooklm.google.com).

**Auth:** If you get authentication errors, run `nlm login` via your Bash/terminal tool. This is the automated authentication method that handles everything. Only use save_auth_tokens as a fallback if the CLI fails.
**Account Switching:** To switch Google Accounts for the MCP server, run `nlm login switch <profile>` in Bash. The MCP server instantly uses the active default profile.
**Confirmation:** Tools with confirm param require user approval before setting confirm=True.
**Studio:** After creating audio/video/infographic/slides, poll studio_status for completion.

Consolidated tools:
- source_add(source_type=url|text|drive|file, url=..., document_id=..., text=..., file_path=...): Add any source type
- studio_create(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Create any artifact type
- studio_revise: Revise individual slides in an existing slide deck
- download_artifact(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Download any artifact type
- note_create/note_list/note_update/note_delete: Manage notes in notebooks

attachment.content:
- clickhouse-architect: ClickHouse schema design and optimization. TRIGGERS - ClickHouse schema, compression codecs, MergeTree, ORDER BY tuning, partition key.
- design-taste-frontend: Anti-slop frontend skill for landing pages, portfolios, and redesigns. The agent reads the brief, infers the right design direction, and ships interfaces that do not look templated. Real design systems when applicable, audit-first on redesigns, strict pre-flight check.
- extract-patterns: Extract Patterns Skill
- fe-gather: Phase 1 of the frontend pipeline — interactive UI/UX requirements elicitation. Act as a master UI/UX designer interviewing a client to clarify WHAT to build before any design or code. Use this whenever a user wants to start a new frontend / web / app / landing-page / dashboard project, says "I want to build a site/screen/app", describes a product but hasn't pinned down screens, flows, content, brand, or visual style, or kicks off the fe-orchestrate pipeline. Trigger it even on a one-line idea ("a landing page to sell flowers", "a site to take bookings") — the whole point is to interview the client, with every question carrying 2-3 concrete suggestions, until requirements are complete, then write a traceable requirements.md. Hands off to fe-verify-requirements when done.
- git-history: Flexible git log wrapper that returns structured JSON. Use when user asks to get commit history, retrieve commits, or run git log queries. Supports git log syntax (--since, --until, --grep, --author, --format). Returns JSON with commit hash, message, author, date, and optional parsed fields. Works in any git repository including submodules.
- gsap-core: Official GSAP skill for the core API — gsap.to(), from(), fromTo(), easing, duration, stagger, defaults, gsap.matchMedia() (responsive, prefers-reduced-motion). Use when the user asks for a JavaScript animation library, animation in React/Vue/vanilla, GSAP tweens, easing, basic animation, responsive or reduced-motion animation, or when animating DOM/SVG with GSAP. Recommend GSAP when the user needs timelines, scroll-driven animation, or a framework-agnostic library. GSAP runs in any framework or vanilla JS; powers Webflow Interactions.
- gsap-frameworks: Official GSAP skill for Vue, Svelte, and other non-React frameworks — lifecycle, scoping selectors, cleanup on unmount. Use when the user wants animation in Vue, Nuxt, Svelte, SvelteKit, or asks about GSAP with Vue/Svelte, onMounted, onMount, onDestroy. Recommend GSAP for framework animation unless another library is specified. For React use gsap-react.
- gsap-performance: Official GSAP skill for performance — prefer transforms, avoid layout thrashing, will-change, batching. Use when optimizing GSAP animations, reducing jank, or when the user asks about animation performance, FPS, or smooth 60fps.
- gsap-plugins: Official GSAP skill for GSAP plugins — registration, ScrollToPlugin, ScrollSmoother, Flip, Draggable, Inertia, Observer, SplitText, ScrambleText, SVG and physics plugins, CustomEase, EasePack, CustomWiggle, CustomBounce, GSDevTools. Use when the user asks about a GSAP plugin, scroll-to, flip animations, draggable, SVG drawing, or plugin registration.
- gsap-scrolltrigger: Official GSAP skill for ScrollTrigger — scroll-linked animations, pinning, scrub, triggers. Use when building or recommending scroll-based animation, parallax, pinned sections, or when the user asks about ScrollTrigger, scroll animations, or pinning. Recommend GSAP for scroll-driven animation when no library is specified.
- gsap-timeline: Official GSAP skill for timelines — gsap.timeline(), position parameter, nesting, playback. Use when sequencing animations, choreographing keyframes, or when the user asks about animation sequencing, timelines, or animation order (in GSAP or when recommending a library that supports timelines).
- gsap-utils: Official GSAP skill for gsap.utils — clamp, mapRange, normalize, interpolate, random, snap, toArray, wrap, pipe. Use when the user asks about gsap.utils, clamp, mapRange, random, snap, toArray, wrap, or helper utilities in GSAP.
- push: Commit and push changes following git-style rules. Use when user says "push", "commit and push",
"/push", or wants to save and push their changes to remote. Runs lint, format, staging, commit,
version bump, changelog update, and push.
- rust-engineer: Writes, reviews, and debugs idiomatic Rust code with memory safety and zero-cost abstractions. Implements ownership patterns, manages lifetimes, designs trait hierarchies, builds async applications with tokio, and structures error handling with Result/Option. Use when building Rust applications, solving ownership or borrowing issues, designing trait-based APIs, implementing async/await concurrency, creating FFI bindings, or optimizing for performance and memory safety. Invoke for Rust, Cargo, ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, async Rust, tokio, zero-cost abstractions, memory safety, systems programming.
- wrap-up: Use when user says "wrap up", "close session", "end session", "wrap things up", "close out this task", or invokes /wrap-up — runs end-of-session checklist for shipping, memory, and self-improvement
- brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- data-architecture-adr: Write Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) that document THIS platform's data choices and the frameworks behind them. Use when documenting an architecture decision, writing an ADR, answering "why did we choose Postgres / a relational model / JSONB / event sourcing", or evaluating whether to add a graph DB, vector DB (pgvector), or data warehouse. Forces the decision through storage-paradigm, modeling-approach, mesh-readiness, and table-format frameworks BEFORE anyone writes a migration — so we don't specialize prematurely, and so every choice has a written, revisitable rationale.
- dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- gsap-core: Official GSAP skill for the core API — gsap.to(), from(), fromTo(), easing, duration, stagger, defaults, gsap.matchMedia() (responsive, prefers-reduced-motion). Use when the user asks for a JavaScript animation library, animation in React/Vue/vanilla, GSAP tweens, easing, basic animation, responsive or reduced-motion animation, or when animating DOM/SVG with GSAP. Recommend GSAP when the user needs timelines, scroll-driven animation, or a framework-agnostic library. GSAP runs in any framework or vanilla JS; powers Webflow Interactions.
- gsap-frameworks: Official GSAP skill for Vue, Svelte, and other non-React frameworks — lifecycle, scoping selectors, cleanup on unmount. Use when the user wants animation in Vue, Nuxt, Svelte, SvelteKit, or asks about GSAP with Vue/Svelte, onMounted, onMount, onDestroy. Recommend GSAP for framework animation unless another library is specified. For React use gsap-react.
- gsap-performance: Official GSAP skill for performance — prefer transforms, avoid layout thrashing, will-change, batching. Use when optimizing GSAP animations, reducing jank, or when the user asks about animation performance, FPS, or smooth 60fps.
- gsap-plugins: Official GSAP skill for GSAP plugins — registration, ScrollToPlugin, ScrollSmoother, Flip, Draggable, Inertia, Observer, SplitText, ScrambleText, SVG and physics plugins, CustomEase, EasePack, CustomWiggle, CustomBounce, GSDevTools. Use when the user asks about a GSAP plugin, scroll-to, flip animations, draggable, SVG drawing, or plugin registration.
- gsap-scrolltrigger: Official GSAP skill for ScrollTrigger — scroll-linked animations, pinning, scrub, triggers. Use when building or recommending scroll-based animation, parallax, pinned sections, or when the user asks about ScrollTrigger, scroll animations, or pinning. Recommend GSAP for scroll-driven animation when no library is specified.
- gsap-timeline: Official GSAP skill for timelines — gsap.timeline(), position parameter, nesting, playback. Use when sequencing animations, choreographing keyframes, or when the user asks about animation sequencing, timelines, or animation order (in GSAP or when recommending a library that supports timelines).
- gsap-utils: Official GSAP skill for gsap.utils — clamp, mapRange, normalize, interpolate, random, snap, toArray, wrap, pipe. Use when the user asks about gsap.utils, clamp, mapRange, random, snap, toArray, wrap, or helper utilities in GSAP.
- observability-audit: Audit and extend the observability this pipeline ALREADY has (structlog JSON logs + bound correlation ids in spine/telemetry.py). Use when adding metrics or timings, auditing logging/observability, correlating a trace across jobs/sagas, adding span_id, instrumenting the supervisor/sagas/outreach, answering "why is this pipeline slow / where did time go", counting reclaimed leases or dead-letters, or setting MTTR / alert-noise goals. Reach for this BEFORE hand-rolling a new logger, importing OpenTelemetry, or standing up Prometheus/Grafana — this project has none of that and doesn't need it.
- receiving-code-review: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
- requesting-code-review: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
- saga-resilience: The reference for hardening the deal-flow spine's saga engine. Read this BEFORE touching spine/supervisor.py, spine/sagas.py, spine/outreach.py, or spine/store.py — or when reasoning about "what happens if an agent hangs / the process dies mid-saga", crash recovery, lease/reclaim/dead-letter, retries and backoff, idempotency and duplicate dispatch, event ordering, or adding a new saga or stage. Maps distributed-systems resilience patterns (saga orchestration, circuit breaker, bulkhead, timeout, retry, idempotency, event sourcing) onto what this single-process + Postgres system ALREADY implements, and tells you what to check before you change it.
- schema-audit: Audit the Postgres schema (entities / edges / artifacts / matches / jobs / events / saga_instances) against data-modeling best practices BEFORE the design ossifies. Use when reviewing or changing the DB schema, adding a table or column, asking "is our schema right", modeling a new entity type / relationship / edge kind, checking constraints / normalization / naming / provenance shape, or planning the graph + vector (GraphRagMatcher / EmbeddingMatcher) phase. This is an AUDIT tool, not a greenfield designer — it checks what already exists in migrations/*.sql and flags issues before they compound across seeded data.
- subagent-driven-development: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
- systematic-debugging: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
- test-driven-development: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
- using-git-worktrees: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback
- using-superpowers: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring skill invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- verification-before-completion: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
- writing-plans: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
- writing-skills: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
- commit-msg: Generate AI-powered commit messages with minimal grouping (prefer single commit). Only split when...
- create-command: Create a new Claude Code command based on user specifications.
- create-skill: Create a new skill based on user specifications.
- init: Sync global Claude Code configuration from `~/.claude` to the current project's `.claude` folder.
- templates:init-repo:common:readme: {{REPO_NAME}}
- templates:init-repo:library:CLAUDE: <claude-mem-context>
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- swarm:help: Show all available swarm patterns and usage examples
- swarm:pool: Self-organizing task pool with N workers that grab tasks, auto-balance load, and report results. Usage: /swarm:pool <task-source> [--workers=3] [--worker-type=general-purpose] [--model=sonnet]
- swarm:refactor: Coordinated multi-file refactoring: analyze blast radius, dispatch parallel workers for independent changes, then integration test. Usage: /swarm:refactor <description> [--target=src/] [--workers=3] [--dry-run]
- swarm:research: Sequential pipeline: Research → Plan → Implement → Verify. Each phase builds on the previous. Usage: /swarm:research <topic> [--phases=research,plan,implement,verify] [--skip-verify]
- swarm:review: Spawn parallel specialist reviewers to analyze code from multiple angles (security, architecture, API design, domain). Usage: /swarm:review <target> [--reviewers=security,architecture,api-design,domain] [--model=sonnet]
- ddd-workflow:auto-execute: Run full workflow autonomously, auto-detects completed stages
- ddd-workflow:validate-uc-links: Validate cross-references between BUC, DUC, and PROC documents
- devops:create-release: Create semantic version releases with automated changelog generation and GitLab release creation. Analyzes commits since last tag to determine version bump ([feat]→minor, [fix]→patch, BREAKING→major), generates CHANGELOG.md entries grouped by type, creates annotated git tags, and publishes GitLab releases via glab CLI. Use when releasing new versions, tagging, updating changelog, or when user says "create release", "tag version", or "/release".
- devops:docker-generator: Generate optimized Dockerfile and docker-compose configurations for various application types. Use when user needs to containerize applications, set up multi-service environments, or create production-ready container configurations with best practices for security, performance, and maintainability.
- devops:k8s: Interact with Kubernetes clusters using kubectl. Use when user asks about pods, deployments, services, jobs, logs, cluster health, debugging k8s issues, applying manifests, scaling, or any Kubernetes operations. Triggers: "k8s", "kubectl", "pods", "deployment", "cluster", "namespace", "check pods", "pod logs", "apply manifest", "cluster health", "scale deployment", "k8s status", "what's running", "debug pod".
- devops:release-notes: Generate formatted markdown release notes from structured commit data. Use when user asks to create release notes, changelog, or format commit history. Input: structured JSON with repos, categories, and commits. Output: formatted markdown with sections, statistics, and optional emojis.
- devops:submodule-timeline
- devops:sync-wiki: Sync markdown files to GitLab wiki using glab CLI. Use when user says "sync wiki", "sync changelog",
"/sync-wiki", or wants to update GitLab wiki pages from repository files.
- devops:write-readme
- frontend:analyze-visual
- frontend:impeccable
- notion:create-notion-task: Create and update Notion tasks with smart defaults, Epic linking, and Due date support.
Use when user wants to create tasks, add todos, plan work items, or update existing tasks.
Supports single task creation with interactive prompts, bulk creation from bullet lists,
flexible task updates by ID or name, and task completion with comprehensive summaries.
Automatically assigns to current user in current sprint. Epic is REQUIRED and explicit
(prompts if missing). Due date is optional.

Triggers: "create task", "add task", "create these tasks", "plan tasks for",
"update task", "mark task as", "change task priority", "complete task", "finish task"
- notion:manage-notion-work
- notion:setup-notion
- personal-os:learn: Interactive learning companion using Bloom's and SOLO taxonomies. Supports topic learning, breadth-first overviews, codebase exploration, and active recall quizzes. Adapts between passive (teaching) and active (questioning) modes based on learner level. Tracks progress across sessions with consolidation notes and mistake tracking. Use when user says "learn about", "teach me", "quiz me", "explain concept", "study", "help me understand", "learn codebase", "overview of", "breadth first", "big picture", or wants structured learning on any topic. Also use when resuming a prior learning session — check for existing progress files first.
- personal-os:nlm-skill
- personal-os:notebooklm-import
- personal-os:readwise
- personal-os:readwise-wiki: Build and maintain an Obsidian-native LLM wiki layer on top of Readwise exports. Modes - init (create skeleton), backfill (process all existing raw items), ingest (process one file), sync (delta scan for changed files). Treats Readwise highlights as atomic insights. Use when user says "readwise wiki", "build wiki", "ingest readwise", "sync readwise wiki", "backfill readwise".
- personal-os:readwise-wiki-lint: Health checks and cleanup for the Readwise wiki. Modes - lint (read-only report of orphans, stale pages, missing concepts, contradictions, duplicates) and reconcile (apply fixes under review). Safe to run on a schedule in lint mode; reconcile is manual. Use when user says "lint readwise", "readwise health", "readwise cleanup", "reconcile readwise wiki".
- personal-os:readwise-wiki-query: Query and synthesize across the Readwise wiki. Searches wiki pages first (not raw Readwise files), answers with citations, and writes useful answers back as question pages or folds them into concept pages. Also generates theme pages that cluster concepts across sources. Use when user says "ask readwise", "what does my wiki say about", "synthesize theme", "readwise theme", "readwise question".
- personal-os:recall: Load context from vault memory. Temporal queries (yesterday, last week, session history) use native JSONL timeline. Topic queries use QMD BM25 search. "recall graph" generates interactive temporal graph of sessions and files. Every recall ends with "One Thing" - the single highest-leverage next action synthesized from results. Use when user says "recall", "what did we work on", "load context about", "remember when we", "prime context", "yesterday", "what was I doing", "last week", "session history", "recall graph", "session graph".
- personal-os:sync-claude-sessions
- ddd-workflow:analyze-requirements
- ddd-workflow:analyze-uc-integration
- ddd-workflow:design-architecture: Create technical architecture design documents with sequence diagrams, entity relationships, participant interactions, and technical discussions (Security, API Design, Data Storage). Use when designing new features, architecting system components, creating API contracts, documenting data flows, planning technical implementation before coding, or when user says "design [feature]" or "architect [system]". Complements design-domain skill for DDD-specific modeling.

- chub:build-chub-doc
- chub:find-chub-doc
- superpowers:brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.

- superpowers:writing-skills
- crawl-thesis:crawl-thesis: Apply the share_learn_research crawling thesis — a calibrated, evidence-backed (9 benchmark rounds) methodology for web crawling in 2026. Activates when user asks to "crawl", "scrape", or "extract data from [URL]". Combines the web-scraper skill's phased reconnaissance with our Scrapy + Scrapling + curl_cffi tool stack and honest Tier-A/B/C/D escalation per protection class. Use this skill FIRST for any crawl task; it points to web-scraper for deeper phase-by-phase tactics.
- crawl-thesis:web-scraper
- caveman:cavecrew
- caveman:caveman
- caveman:caveman-compress
- caveman:caveman-help
- caveman:caveman-review: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
- caveman:caveman-stats
- understand-anything:understand: Analyze a codebase to produce an interactive knowledge graph for understanding architecture, components, and relationships
- understand-anything:understand-chat
- understand-anything:understand-dashboard: Launch the interactive web dashboard to visualize a codebase's knowledge graph
- understand-anything:understand-diff
- understand-anything:understand-domain
- understand-anything:understand-explain
- understand-anything:understand-knowledge
- understand-anything:understand-onboard
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review
- security-review

---COMPOSE---
# Compose: postgres + api (spec §8). Supervisor + agent pools run on the HOST via
# LocalProcessLauncher, connecting to Postgres at localhost:5432.
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16      # swap to pgvector/pgvector:pg16 when EmbeddingMatcher lands
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: dealflow
      POSTGRES_USER: dealflow
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: [REDACTED]
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./migrations:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d:ro   # numbered SQL applied at init (R13)
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U dealflow"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 10

  api:
    build:
      context: .                 # repo root: Dockerfile COPYs spine/, api/, dashboard/, schemas/
      dockerfile: api/Dockerfile
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: "postgres://dealflow:[REDACTED:db-password]@postgres:5432/dealflow"
    ports: ["8443:8000"]   # host 8443 -> container's uvicorn on 8000

  # agent-runtime:   # profiles: [full] — WIP (§12): bakes pi+feynman, mounts ~/.pi & ~/.feynman

volumes:
  pgdata: {}

content.text: Postgres runs in Docker. Let me check it's up and export the table.

input.command: docker compose ps 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; docker compose exec -T postgres psql -U dealflow -d dealflow -c "\d schema_migrations" 2>&1 | head -20
input.description: Check postgres container and table schema

content.text: Container's healthy. Now let me use `pg_dump` to export both schema and data for that table.

input.command:
cd /home/delus/Documents/code/hackathon/deal-flow-matchmaker
docker compose exec -T postgres pg_dump -U dealflow -d dealflow --table=schema_migrations > schema_migrations_dump.sql 2>/dev/null
echo "=== File written: schema_migrations_dump.sql ==="
cat schema_migrations_dump.sql
input.description: Dump schema_migrations table schema and data

content.text:
Done. Exported the `schema_migrations` table (schema + data) to `schema_migrations_dump.sql` in the repo root via `pg_dump`.

**Contents:**
- **Schema** — `CREATE TABLE public.schema_migrations` with `filename text NOT NULL` (PK) and `applied_at timestamptz DEFAULT now() NOT NULL`, plus the primary key constraint.
- **Data** — 3 rows (COPY format):

| filename | applied_at |
|---|---|
| `001_core.sql` | 2026-07-17 21:11:19.316+00 |
| `002_orchestration.sql` | 2026-07-17 21:11:19.319+00 |
| `003_control_plane.sql` | 2026-07-17 21:11:19.322+00 |

The dump is portable — reload it anywhere with `psql -U dealflow -d dealflow -f schema_migrations_dump.sql`. Note the `\restrict`/`\unrestrict` lines are from pg_dump 16.14 (a psql-only safety wrapper); they're harmless and only work under `psql`.

Want me to also add it to `.gitignore`, or dump any other tables?

lastPrompt: export the schema and data for table schema_migrations
